coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained unprecedented attention as a novel luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial owing to their diverse industrial applications. Herein, we reported the sustainable synthesis of fluorescent CDs from papaya peel waste, acting as a natural carbon originator. As-prepared CDs and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) were fabricated in the composites through a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Synthesized RGO/CDs (RC) nanocomposites were characterized using spectroscopic, diffraction, and electron-microscopic techniques. Nanocomposites with variable RGO to CD mass ratios were tested for photodegradation of textile dye methylene blue (MB). The highest photocatalytic activity (degradation efficiency of 87% in 135 min) was obtained in the nanocomposite containing a 2꞉1 mass ratio (RC2). The RGO sheets in the nanocomposite acted as media for electron acceptors, promoting the fast transfer and separation of photoinduced electrons during CDs excitation, thus preventing the recombination of the electron and holes. Based on the agar well diffusion assay, the nanocomposites exhibited excellent antibacterial activity than other tested materials against Bacillus subtilis (Gram-positive) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram-negative) bacterium. The largest inhibition zone area (22 mm), i.e., the highest antimicrobial activity, was obtained in the nanocomposite tested against Gram-positive strains. Taken together, the synergistic effect of RGO and CDs enhanced the photocatalytic and antibacterial performance of synthesized nanocomposite material.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained unprecedented attention as a novel luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial owing to their diverse industrial applications. Herein, we reported the sustainable synthesis of fluorescent CDs from papaya peel waste, acting as a natural carbon originator. As-prepared CDs and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) were fabricated in the composites through a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Synthesized RGO/CDs (RC) nanocomposites were characterized using spectroscopic, diffraction, and electron-microscopic techniques. Nanocomposites with variable RGO to CD mass ratios were tested for photodegradation of textile dye methylene blue (MB). The highest photocatalytic activity (degradation efficiency of 87% in 135 min) was obtained in the nanocomposite containing a 2꞉1 mass ratio (RC2). The RGO sheets in the nanocomposite acted as media for electron acceptors, promoting the fast transfer and separation of photoinduced electrons during CDs excitation, thus preventing the recombination of the electron and holes. Based on the agar well diffusion assay, the nanocomposites exhibited excellent antibacterial activity than other tested materials against Bacillus subtilis (Gram-positive) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram-negative) bacterium. The largest inhibition zone area (22 mm), i.e., the highest antimicrobial activity, was obtained in the nanocomposite tested against Gram-positive strains. Taken together, the synergistic effect of RGO and CDs enhanced the photocatalytic and antibacterial performance of synthesized nanocomposite material.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination and environmental pollution are critical problems worldwide due to the growing use of organic compounds, especially in the agricultural industry. The discharge of untreated or poorly treated coloured wastewater to water bodies is considered a serious public health concern. Dyes are among the most concerning organic pollutants that adversely impact human health, the aquatic ecosystem, and the environment (Yao et al., 2020; Bushra et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021). With an annual production of 700 000 t, over 10 000 types of commercial textile dyes are released into water streams without proper treatment. The textile industry is a significant consumer of dyes, as it alone consumes up to 50% of the total dyes. Therefore, it is imperative to treat colored contaminated effluents to supply clean and safe water for different purposes and develop sanitation to support sustainable development goals (Bushra et al., 2016). Untreated hazardous releases from the numerous bleaching and dyeing industries have severely polluted water and soils, threatening the environment and posing a severe health risk to human health. It is essential to treat wastewater properly to ensure the purity of our environment through an efficient and economic strategy (Singha et al., 2021). Environmental pollution can be treated using several methods of recycling technology that can recover valuable materials from waste and the catalytic converters on engine exhaust systems that can reduce toxic emissions. Photocatalysis technology has been found to be effective in air purification, sewage treatment, cleaning, and sterilization (Katheresan et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2021). Photocatalysts degrade a broad spectrum of toxic compounds under solar irradiation, thereby significantly reducing environmental problems.

Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials with high photocatalytic degradation capability and adsorption properties have been demonstrated to effectively reduce the organic contaminants in air and water (Zou et al., 2019; Ajiboye et al., 2021). Among carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene oxide (GO) and carbon dots (CDs) are favorable components for photocatalytic applications. The coupling of GO with carbon nanomaterials (e.g., CDs) to construct a reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/CD composite photocatalyst is a potential strategy for improving the photocatalytic performance of CDs, because it can increase the range of light response, enhance the mobility of photoinduced charge carriers (electron and holes), and inhibit their recombination. The CDs are defined as small carbon nanoparticles with good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, high quantum yield, low cytotoxicity, and outstanding photostability. In recent years, numerous novel CDs based photocatalysts have been produced due to their intriguing characteristics and distinctive structure. As CDs display tunable and robust fluorescence emission properties, their application in other fields, such as bio-sensing and bioimaging, are rapidly becoming an object of interest among researchers (Wang et al., 2016; Kang et al., 2020; De Oliveira and Da Silva Abreu, 2021). Production of CDs from bio-precursors is of utmost importance due to inexpensive raw materials and eco-friendly production methods. Biomass waste is considered an excellent innocuous carbon source for producing CDs. Attempts have been made to synthesize biomass wastes derived CDs for various industrial applications (Kang et al., 2020). Compared to synthetically derived CDs, biomass-derived CDs do not require surface passivation because of the presence of carbonaceous and heteroatom-containing compounds. The CDs and functionalized CDs are generally synthesized by laser ablation, arc-discharge, ultra-sonication, microwave, and hydrothermal methods. The hydrothermal process is generally preferred for synthesizing CDs from natural bio-waste (Salimi Shahraki et al., 2022). Thionyl chloride-doped bio-CDs with a high degree of crystallinity were produced hydrothermally from palm veneer as the carbon source. The multiple surface defect states, especially the surface dangling bonds play a very important role in the photocatalysis of CDs. The impact of the heteroatom doping strategy is linked to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs. In biomass-derived CDs, co-doping with Cl and S produces different defect states that prohibit excited electrons from returning to the ground state and restrict the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited superior photocatalytic performance towards the degradation of various dyes (MB and Rhodamine-B) with a removal efficiency of 72.0% and 94.2%, respectively (Zhu et al., 2020).

Similarly, CDs originated from Dunaliella salina (green algae), co-doped with nitrogen/sulfur, were successfully utilized for the removal of MB and methyl violet. The carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs promote the separation and transfer of electrons and holes, increasing the photocatalytic activity. In addition, holes on the surface of the material are helpful in the degradation of pollutants. The photodegradation rate of doped CDs was two times higher as compared to the un-doped form (Li et al., 2019b). Surprisingly, hydrothermally synthesized green-emissive CDs synthesized from pear juice demonstrated excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation due to their efficient light absorption, electron transfer, and capability of separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Synthesized CDs degraded 99.5% of MB dye in 130 min (Das et al., 2019). The TiO2 modified nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) prepared from spent coffee grounds demonstrated excellent photocatalytic degradation of MB dye (93.1%) within 60 min (Jin et al., 2023). Similarly, a waste oil palm frond (Heng et al., 2022), corncob (Xie et al., 2021), waste sludge, and lignin-based CDs and their composite (Ma et al., 2022) were utilized for the photocatalytic decomposition of the pollutants.

Graphene is the elusive two-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with high potential in numerous fields. By incorporating graphene into complex assemblies, many potential applications can be accomplished (Smith et al., 2019; Ławkowska et al., 2021). Many graphene-based composite materials with remarkable properties have been applied in the energy and environmental sectors (Zeng et al., 2018; Asadzadeh-Khaneghah and Habibi-Yangjeh, 2020; Facure et al., 2021). Recently, several studies reported that the anchoring of CDs on RGO could be utilized as electrocatalysts (Zhao et al., 2019), supercapacitors (Li et al., 2019a), and fluorescent materials (Zheng et al., 2018).

Herein, the RGO/CDs nanocomposite and CDs from papaya peel waste were hydrothermally synthesized without any surface passivation. Papaya has been chosen, as papaya leaves and peels have shown great potential for the preparation of CDs (Gudimella et al., 2022). Previously, papaya products have been utilized in adsorbing dyes from textile wastewater (Krishni et al., 2014). The heteroatoms doped on graphene framework were provided by papaya-based biomass. Using cheap precursors and ecofriendly methods for preparation of the CDs are important stepping stones toward environmental management. Different analytical techniques were employed to analyze the CDs and their composite materials. The RGO/CDs nanocomposite was tested in the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye under visible light. Compared to pristine CDs, RGO/CDs composites showed considerable improvement in dye degradation activity due to high chemical stability and the large specific surface area of RGO. Under visible light, CDs exhibit low separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers, thus decreasing the photocatalytic activity. The CDs were anchored on graphene sheets to improve photocatalytic performance and impede the high electron-hole recombination rate, which can significantly speed up the separation of charge carriers (Phang and Tan, 2019). In addition, the antibacterial activity of the CDs and the composite material has been evaluated against two bacterial species, Bacillus subtilis, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, using the agar well diffusion method.

Ethanol (C2H5OH) and acetone (C3H6O) were purchased from Merck, India. Methylene blue was procured from Fisher Scientific, India, and graphite powder was obtained from Sigma Aldrich, India. All the other chemicals and reagents were used as received without further purification. Double distilled water (DDW) was used for dilution and washing purposes. The papaya was purchased from the local vegetable market (Civil lines, Dodhpur) in Aligarh, India.

The CDs from papaya peel were prepared using a hydrothermal approach. The peels were chopped down, and then heated overnight in an oven at 80 ℃ to eliminate moisture. Thereafter, 8 g of dried powder was mixed with 100 mL of distilled water. This mixture was placed in an autoclave (Teflon-lined stainless steel) and heated for 24 h at 200 ℃. Dark brown solutions of CDs were obtained after filtering through cellulose ester membranes. The CDs were kept in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further use (Wang et al., 2016).

The suspensions of GO were prepared by the modified Hummers method from graphite powder (Zaaba et al., 2017). The composites (RGO/CDs) were synthesized hydrothermally, as shown in Fig. 1 . Varying amounts of GO mixed with the 8 mL CDs solution were subjected to ultrasonic vibration for 30 min, then moved into an autoclave (Teflon lined stainless-steel) and kept at 150 ℃ for 4 h. Finally, the composite (RGO/CDs) obtained through filtration were washed several times with DDW and then freeze-dried at −40 ℃ overnight (Hoang et al., 2019). Composites with different GO꞉CDs mass ratios (4꞉1, 2꞉1, and 1꞉1) were synthesized and labelled as RC3, RC2, and RC1, respectively, where R and C refer to RGO and CDs accordingly.

The X-ray diffraction technique (PW-1148/89 Netherlands with CuKa radiation) was used to confirm the presence of graphitic structure in the synthesized materials. Figure 2 displayed a prominent peak at 2θ = 26.7° relative to (002) peak (hexagonal structure lattice) that has been compatible with the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) Card No. 75-0444 (Chen et al., 2018), and 43.3°(101) corresponding to the diffraction of the graphitic framework of RGO in composites (Xu et al., 2010). In addition, the interlayer spacing of RGO (0.366 nm) was much lower than GO, suggesting the successful reduction of GO to RGO. The characteristic peak of CDs at 2θ = 23° relative to the (311) plane of carbon (face-centered cubic crystal structure of CDs) (Thambiraj and Ravi Shankaran, 2016) was weak or disappeared in the composites due to the weak diffraction intensity and less content of CDs in the RGO. Inclusion of CDs is beneficial for recovering the π-conjugated system of RGO (Zhao et al., 2017). Furthermore, the crystallite size of CDs, RC1, RC2, and RC3, as determined from the Debye Sherrer equation, was found to be 4.02, 7.02, 7.07, and 8.09 nm, respectively.

Functional groups on CDs and their composites were assessed using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The measurements were made using a Perkin Elimer FT-IR spectrometer (Model Spectrum-BX, USA) by the KBr disc method. The FT-IR spectra of CDs depicted the existence of various functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 3b). The strong absorption band exhibited by CDs at 3 370 cm−1 was associated with the O–H stretching vibrations. Besides, the peaks observed at 2 962, 1 656, and 1 386 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibrations of C–H (stretching), C=C (stretching), and N–H (bending), respectively (Bokare et al., 2020). The absorbance band at 1 068 cm–1 was due to the C–N stretching in CDs. In addition, the peaks in the region (1 465–1 050 cm–1) are related to the presence of epoxy C–O, and the symmetric stretching vibration of COO–, respectively. The presence of various functional groups in CDs enhanced their aqueous solubility. Based on the literature reports (Ma et al., 2016; Shahid et al., 2017), the characteristic absorption peaks of GO was observed at 3 400 (O–H), 2 927 (C–H), 1 781 (C=O), 1 658 (C=C), 1 426 (C–O), 1 230 and 1 118 cm−1 (alkoxy C–O stretch) GO as shown in Fig. 3 a. The intensity of the respective peaks corresponding to the functional groups containing oxygen of GO (C=O, COO–, C–O–C, and –OH) are either weak or disappeared in the composite. This indicates the formation of RGO in the composite material. The spectra of RGO/CDs composites represent the similar characteristic peaks of the CDs, which ensures that the composites not only have the structural characteristics of RGO but also own the desired vibrations/components of CDs, which are beneficial for the successful application of the material in managing the water pollution. It is worth mentioning that the reducing activity of the OH group on the CDs surface demonstrated the electron donation potential of CDs during the formation of the composite (Alarfaj et al., 2018).

The in-depth morphologies of the as-prepared CDs and their nanocomposite were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (TEM 2100, JEOL, Japan microscope operated at 200 kV) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM, 6510LV, JEOL, Japan microscope). Generally, in the hydrothermally synthesized nanocomposite, the presence of CDs inhibits the graphene sheets from being restacked. It leads to an increase in the surface area and pore volume, which promote the penetration of ions and, in turn, improve the photocatalytic performance of the composite material compared with those of pure reduced graphene oxide (Hoang et al., 2019). Figures 4 a and b show the TEM image of the CDs and their composite. CDs were well anchored on the graphene sheet uniformly, indicating the good combination of graphene and CDs. The average size distribution of CDs, as determined using Image-J software, was in the range from 1 to 8 nm, with a maximum population at 4.5 nm, suggesting that the CDs had a narrow size distribution (Fig. 4c). Further evidence for the attachment of CDs onto the graphene was provided by the SEM analysis (Figs. 5 a, b) which displayed the aggregation of quasi-spherical CDs on the typically wrinkled sheets of RGO.

Figure 6 depicts the Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra of CDs, RC1, RC2, and RC3 nanocomposites. The CDs exhibited peaks at 221 and 332 nm, typically ascribed to the π→π* and n→π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains (–C=C–, and –C–C–) of graphitic core and the sp3 hybridized domains (C=O, C–N or –C–OH) originate from carboxyl/amine groups available on the surface of CDs (Emam et al., 2017). Previously, it has been reported that the GO absorption band at 232 and 290 nm represented the π→π* and n→π* electronic transitions of C=C bonds (Yashwanth et al., 2020). For RGO, it has been reported that these two peaks completely disappeared, and a new broad peak appeared at a higher wavelength (282 nm) (Hareesh et al., 2016). The RGO/CD nanocomposites displayed two peaks at 221 nm and 282 nm, which are the characteristic peaks of CDs, and RGO, respectively. It could be seen from Fig. 6 that absorbance of the nanocomposites at 282 nm increased compared to the CDs due to the absorption contribution of RGO. As the concentration of RGO increases, peak intensity increased because more radiations were absorbed and absorbance went up (hyperchromic effect); the absorbance was directly proportional to the concentration according to the Beers law.

Figure 7 a shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs, RC1, RC2, and RC3 nanocomposites. The CDs showed an emission peak at 470 nm, associated with the recombination of photogenerated excitons. In contrast to CDs, RC nanocomposites show a lower PL emission intensity because of the quenching effect signifying the effective charge transport at the CDs/RGO interface (Phang and Tan, 2019). The electron-accepting features of RGO could contribute to the suppression of the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Dang et al., 2018). The PL of the composite at various excitation wavelengths (290–410 nm) showed an increase in the peak intensity with the increased wavelength (Fig. 7b). The excitation wavelength-dependent emission red shift was observed in the material as the excitation wavelength increased (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). Surface-attached functional groups of the nanocomposite created multiple discrete electronic states responsible for the excitation-dependent emission behavior (Sharma et al., 2016). The fluorescence of CDs/GO-based composite resulted from transitions related to the structural disorder-induced localized states of the sp2 π—π* gap (Chien et al., 2012).

Figure S1 shows the UV-vis DRS spectrum of the CDs and RC2 nanocomposite material using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (DRS). It was evident that, compared to CDs, the composite exhibited the increased light absorption intensity in the visible region due to the presence of RGO, ascribed to the operating electronic interactions between RGO and CDs (Dang et al., 2018).

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite materials was evaluated by studying the decomposition of methylene blue (MB) dye (1 × 10–5) in an immersion well reactor of Pyrex glass under visible light illumination (500 W halogen lamp and 9 500 lumens). The 180 mg of 1 g/L catalyst was transferred to the photoreactor containing 180 mL of dye (1 × 10–5) solution, constantly stirred with the supply of air, and cooled by circulating cold water to avoid any thermal reaction. The mixture was kept under stirring for adsorption–desorption equilibration. After every 15 min of irradiation, 5 mL of MB solution was withdrawn from the reactor at regular intervals and then centrifuged (5 000 r/min) to remove the particles. Finally, the samples were analyzed using a UV-vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, lambda 35). The change in the optical intensity of the dye was determined at the λ max of MB (MB, 663 nm), at various irradiation times. The percentage degradation of the samples was evaluated using Eq. (1) (Bushra et al., 2014).

where C 0 and C t are the initial and the final concentration of the dye at t 0 and t, respectively.

Hydrophilicity and dispersibility are the two essential parameters that greatly influence the photocatalysis process. The CDs can freely disperse in water and exhibit good photostability, i.e., luminescence properties (Li et al., 2010). Distinctive fluorescence nature and photoinduced electron-transfer behaviour are required for any material to act as a photocatalyst (Cui et al., 2021). The upconverted PL properties of CDs are beneficial in the degradation of pollutants. Additionally, GO produces the adsorption sites to pollutants and functions as an electron mediator and catalytic support. A synergetic effect between CDs and RGO is responsible for enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activity was determined by irradiating the aqueous suspension of dye in the presence of the CDs and the nanocomposite material using a visible light source with continuous bubbling of atmospheric oxygen. The decomposition was found to follow the spectrophotometric measurement of the intensity of the absorption spectrum. Figure 8 a shows the change in the optical spectra of MB at their maximum absorbance in the presence of the photocatalysts under visible light. With the increase in the illumination time, the absorption maxima gradually decreased due to the gradual breakdown of the chromophoric group in the dye (Yin et al., 2014). Figure 8b depicts the concentration change of MB dye in the presence of CDs and different RGO/CDs nanocomposites at different illumination times. In the absence of a photocatalyst, no visible depletion in MB content was observed. While for CDs, RC1, RC2, and RC3, 75%, 78%, 87%, and 67% degradation were determined, respectively. This indicated the significance of tailoring the composite structures in designing the photocatalyst. In contrast to the pristine CDs, the highest photocatalytic activity was obtained for RC2 with RGO꞉CDs ratios of 2꞉1. This was in line with the increase of photocatalytic activity with increasing RGO content in the samples. Furthermore, RGO in the nanocomposite served as a medium for electron transfer, restraining the recombination of electrons and holes and extending the lifetime of photogenerated electrons (Lightcap et al., 2010). The percentage of dye degradation was decreased in RC3 due to blocking active sites on the catalyst. Thus, this led to light obstruction and restricted the generation of charge carriers in the catalyst.

The degradation mechanism of dye in the presence of nanocomposite could be explained as follows: under visible light irradiation, CDs produced electrons in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) state, which could be transferred easily to the RGO due to the good interfacial interaction between CDs and RGO. Moreover, these photogenerated electrons would move by the restored sp2 hybridized carbon domains of RGO sheets by the convenient extra path, thereby suppressing the electron–hole pair recombination rate. Finally, these electrons would combine with the oxygen molecules (electron acceptors), facilitating the formation of superoxide radicals that effectively led to MB dye degradation under visible light. The increased surface area of the nanocomposites, associated with the anchoring of CDs on RGO sheets, which prevented their restacking, was contributed to their more significant photocatalytic activity. In addition, RGO sheets prevented the agglomeration of CDs, enhancing the surface area of the nanocomposite materials, which resulted in the increased adsorption of dye molecules (Ashritha et al., 2021) (Fig. S2). A comparison study was made with relevant data from other works. Compared to the activity reported for other materials, the synthesized composite had one of the highest activities against MB under visible light illumination (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters for RC2 nanocomposite
Effect of initial dye concentration on dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of dye concentration on the de-colorization rate of MB dye was examined by changing the initial dye concentration (10–50 mg/L). The findings showed a decrease in the degradation rate with the increase in dye concentrations (Fig. S3). The optimum dye concentration was found to be 10 mg/L. At high concentrations, photons are intercepted by the dye molecules before reaching the surface of catalyst, resulting in lower production of hydroxy radicals and in turn slowing down the degradation process (Reza et al., 2017), while at a lower concentration, more active sites and hydroxyl radicals existed to destroy the MB molecule. The decolorization rate depended on hydroxyl radical formation, a critical species in the photocatalysis process.

The effect of the amount of (0.25–2.00 g/L) catalyst on the degradation rate was examined. As observed in Fig. S4, the degradation rate of MB was increased from 70.5% to 88.0% when the catalyst amount was raised from 0.25 to 1.00 g/L. This increased rate was due to the increased amount of RC2, which enhanced the photons absorption by the composite material. This resulted in an increased production of reactive oxygen species and significantly contributing to dye degradation. On further increase in the catalytic dosage, the degradation rate was slightly increased. Generally, a large amount of the catalyst increased suspension turbidity, decreasing the penetration of light and making the degradation process less effective (Fadllan et al., 2017).

As far as the practical application of the fabricated photocatalyst is concerned, stability and reproducibility play crucial roles. Therefore, to estimate the stability of the composite, we investigated four-cycle tests by incorporating recovered catalysts into MB dye solutions under comparable conditions. The materials were collected and washed with water/ethanol before using for the next cycle. From Fig. 9 , it was established that the nanocomposite could be recycled and reused without any appreciable decrease in degradation rate, maintaining the degradation rate of 82% after four photocatalytic cycles. This pinpointed the importance of the designed photocatalyst in the environmental remediation of the targeted pollutants with high reusability potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM and XRD after photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the nanocomposite (RC2) was further confirmed by the SEM and XRD analysis. It can be seen from Fig. S5 that the recovered photocatalyst in XRD showed almost the same patterns as the initial one, which justified that the nanocomposite had high stability. Before catalysis, the composite had a well-defined nanostructured surface, while the surface of the recovered one was coarse with a shallower protrusion, as demonstrated in the SEM images (Fig. S6). Moreover, the surface structure of both did not have any significant morphological changes, demonstrating the stability of the nanocomposite material.

The nature of the adsorption process was determined by fitting the MB data to the Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherms. The experiments were carried out by stirring a water suspension of the MB dye (5×10–6–3 ×10–5) in a closed vessel with a catalyst (1 g) at room temperature for 24 h in the dark. The Langmuir isotherm is associated with the monolayer adsorption onto the homogeneous surface, while the Freundlich isotherm is associated with multilayer adsorption on heterogeneous surfaces (Ahmad and Ansari, 2020).

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm model is calculated by using Eqs. (2) and (3):
(2)

The Freundlich (Eqs. (4) and (5)) isotherm model is expressed as:
(4)

and the linearized form of the Freundlich isotherm model is:

where q e quantifies the quantity of dye per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g); q m is the medium's maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g); C e (mg/L) is the dye concentration at equilibrium, K L (L/mg) is the Langmuir equilibrium constant, while the values of n and K F are the linearity index and Freundlich constants respectively, which are related to the adsorption capacity.

The study of the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms demonstrated the effectiveness of the adsorption process. The surface areas of CDs were heterogeneous, making them appropriate for both effective monolayer and multilayer stacking following both adsorption processes. In the present work, the adsorption isotherms fitted well with both Freundlich and Langmuir for MB, as shown in Figs. 10 a and 10b, and their corresponding parameters were mentioned in Table 2 . The results revealed that the adsorption of MB onto the RC2 nanocomposite gave a closer fitting with Freundlich isotherm with a high coefficient of determination R 2 (0.985 5), better than the Langmuir isotherm model (0.927 7). The Freundlich isotherm is suitable for non-ideal sorption, involving heterogeneous surfaces (Abidi et al., 2019). It was assumed that there were neither homogeneous site energies nor a limited level of sorption (Alqadami et al., 2018). From the plot of ln q e against ln C e (Fig. 10a), the values of n (determined from slope), K F (determined from intercept), and R 2 (coefficient of correlation) were found to be 1.095,1.980, and 0.985 5, respectively. The value of 1/n, which lied between zero and one, signified the surface uniformity or adsorption intensity. As the value of n gets closer to zero, the surface becomes homogeneous (Aji et al., 2017). It was observed from the results that the value of 1/n is 1.095, demonstrating heterogeneity of the surface and an exponential distribution of nanocomposite active sites. (Silas et al., 2020). In this study, the exponent n value was more than one, suggesting that the process of dye adsorption on the nanocomposite was likely to be favorable.

The kinetic plot obtained by plotting ln (C 0/C t) against different time intervals for degradation of MB dye depicted a linear correlation, suggesting that removing dye using CDs and its composites as catalysts followed pseudo-first-order kinetics. The first-order kinetics was determined by using the Eq. (6) (Simonin, 2016):

where k app is the apparent rate constant, and C 0 and C t are the initial and the final concentration of the dye at t 0 and t, respectively (min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of CDs, RGO and nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: For a material to work as an antimicrobial agent, it should be water-soluble. Water solubility will aid in providing an appropriate liquid composition that will successfully transfer the antibacterial agent to the substrate. The hydrophilic nature is considered an essential prerequisite for any antimicrobial agent to show activity (Jain et al., 2014). The CDs and GO are hydrophilic with excellent stability in the solution due to oxygen functionalities at their surface, which provides a base for their application as an antimicrobial agent. The antibacterial potential of CDs, RGO, and RC nanocomposite was analyzed against two bacterial species, namely Bacillus subtilis CD001 (+) (Gram-positive; B.S) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa MTCC-424 (-) (Gram-negative; P.A). The sources of B. subtilis and P. aeruginosa are soil and water, respectively. These strains of bacteria were procured from the Department of Biochemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, India. The well diffusion method was used for antibacterial assessment. Equal concentration of 0.3 mg/mL was taken from all three samples, and about 100 µL of the freshly grown culture of bacteria was spread on plates. The plates were then incubated for 24 h at 37 ℃. All the synthesized materials showed excellent antibacterial activity against two bacterial species. The digital images of the microbial colonies were shown in Figs. 12 a and b. Compared to CDs (B.S = 18 mm, P.A = 13 mm) and RGO (B.S = 20 mm, P.A =16 mm), the highest activity (large area of the zone of inhibition) was observed in the nanocomposite (B.S = 22 mm, P.A = 18 mm). At the same time, a large inhibitory effect was observed against the gram-positive stains (Fig. 12c and Table S1). In contrast to B.A, P.A showed a lower inhibition effect towards the nanocomposite, which was attributed to the resistance of the outer structure of membrane of the gram –ve bacteria that hindered any antibiotics/material from passing through its passages effortlessly, while gram +ve bacteria lacked this layer (Miller, 2016; Exner et al., 2017). Therefore, the P.A was more resistant to antibiotics/materials than B.S. The inhibitory or antibacterial effect of the synthesized product was due to the excellent synergism between CDs and RGO, in which the presence of a π-conjugated system was easily coupled with the electron transfer mechanism of the bacterial cell wall through electrostatic interaction. It was reported that these bacteria could effectively interact with the hydroxyl radicals and superoxide produced by CDs (Ghirardello et al., 2021). In addition, CDs bore a positive charge because some adhered biomolecules, which could interact with the negatively charged bacterial cell wall, deeply penetrated its membrane and disturbed the metabolic function, which in turn led to the death of microbes (Dong et al., 2017; Das et al., 2020; Malik and Javed, 2021). Therefore, the CDs synthesized nanocomposite could be a potential candidate for pharmaceutical applications.

Initially, the mechanism involved the adhesion of the material to the bacterial cell wall, followed by the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which involved the generation of free electrons that interacted with various molecules in the cell to produce free radicals. These radicals attacked the DNA and blocked its replication process, and then proteins were damaged by these free radicals and oxidized, thus impairing the metabolic function of microbes. The vicious cycle continues, resulting in the killing of the bacterial population (Fig. S7). In general, CDs inhibited bacterial growth through a complex mechanism. The antibacterial potential of CD was highly dependent on the surface charge and generation of reactive oxide species. Besides, ROS production by GO-based composites was also responsible for increasing the antibacterial potential of the material. A multivalent interaction between RC nanocomposites and the bacterial membrane resulted in significant membrane damage and cytoplasm leakage (Li et al., 2016). Additionally, the RC could influence the expression of genes by interacting with plasmid deoxyribonucleic acid and short-interfering ribonucleic acid.

Papaya biomass-derived CDs and their different composites (RC1, RC2, and RC3) were successfully developed by a facile hydrothermal method for the visible-light degradation of MB dye and antibacterial applications. The photocatalytic performance was investigated as a function of RGO content. Compared to the pristine CDs, the composite material (RC2) showed a remarkable photocatalytic activity for the degradation of MB dye due to the synergistic effect of CDs and RGO. In addition, the RC2 composite exhibited sufficient stability in the photodegradation of the dye with a negligible decrease in the degradation efficiency after three consecutive runs. We also found that nanocomposites demonstrated a better bactericidal effect than their counterparts. The RGO/CDs nanocomposite affected the membrane integrity more in gram positive bacterium than in gram negative, which opened up avenues for its use as an effective antimicrobial. The CDs and RGO were promising antibacterial nanomaterials due to their easy process of production and functionalization, high water solubility, and apparent biocompatibility. This work not only demonstrated the potential of the material for multipurpose applications but also provided new insight into utilizing low-cost waste in a more sustainable manner. Besides, it also established the role of CDs and RGO in enhancing the photocatalytic and antimicrobial activities in the nanocomposite material.
